revision.text:
{{神社 |名称=二所山田神社 |所在地=山口県周南市大字鹿野上2894 |祭神=八千矛神・大物主神（二所神社）
天照大神・豊受大神（山田神社） |社格=式内社（二所神社）・国史見在社（山田神社）・郷社 |本殿=[[流造]] }} '''二所山田神社'''（にしょやまだじんじゃ）は、[[山口県]][[周南市]]にある[[神社]]である。[[式内社]]で、旧社格は[[郷社]]。 明治時代に二所神社と山田神社が[[神社合祀|合併]]してできた神社で、旧二所神社の祭神は[[大国主|八千矛神]]・[[大物主|大物主神]]、旧山田神社の祭神は[[天照大神]]・[[トヨウケビメ|豊受大神]]である。 当社が設立した月刊新聞社「女子道社」は、日本全国の社寺で授与される[[おみくじ]]の約6割を製造している。 ==歴史== 二所神社は、旧称を'''二所大明神'''といい、社伝によればは[[昌泰]]2年（[[899年]]）の創建である。[[弘長]]2年（[[1262年]]）の社録に「二所二俣神社」と記載されており、『延喜式神名帳』に記される「[[周防国]][[都濃郡]] 二俣神社」の論社の一社となっている（他に論社が2社ある）。[[大内氏]]が[[元亨]]元年（[[1321年]]）に社房を建立し、[[毛利氏]]は御祈祷所に指定している。明治初年には郷社に列格した。 山田神社は、旧称を'''山田権前社'''という。伊勢国山田から勧請を受けて創建されたと伝えられ、『[[日本文徳天皇実録|文徳実録]]』元慶2年6月23日条に「周防国正六位上赤田神山田神并授従五位下」と記載される国史見在社である（ただし、この「山田神」については[[大島郡]][[周防大島町]]の[[山田神社 (周防大島町)|山田神社]]も論社となっている）。明治初年には村社に列格した。 明治40年（[[1907年]]）に二所神社と山田神社が合併し、現社名になった。 明治時代の当社宮司・宮本重胤は、女性の自立のための全国組織「敬神婦人会」を設立した。明治39年（[[1906年]]）にその機関誌『女子道』を発刊し、その資金源としておみくじの製造が開始され、現在はトップシェアとなっている。また、おみくじの自動頒布機を考案したのも同社である。 {{デフォルトソート:にしよやまたしんしや}} [[Category:山口県の神社]] [[Category:式内小社]] [[Category:国史見在社]] [[Category:府県社]]

{{神社 |名称=二所山田神社 |所在地=山口県周南市大字鹿野上2894 |祭神=八千矛神・大物主神（二所神社）
天照大神・豊受大神（山田神社） |社格=式内社（二所神社）・国史見在社（山田神社）・郷社 |本殿=[[流造]] }} '''二所山田神社'''（にしょやまだじんじゃ）は、[[山口県]][[周南市]]にある[[神社]]である。[[式内社]]で、旧社格は[[郷社]]。 明治時代に二所神社と山田神社が[[神社合祀|合併]]してできた神社で、旧二所神社の祭神は[[大国主|八千矛神]]・[[大物主|大物主神]]、旧山田神社の祭神は[[天照大神]]・[[トヨウケビメ|豊受大神]]である。 当社が設立した月刊新聞社「女子道社」は、日本全国の社寺で授与される[[おみくじ]]の約6割を製造している。 ==歴史== 二所神社は、旧称を'''二所大明神'''といい、社伝によればは[[昌泰]]2年（[[899年]]）の創建である。[[弘長]]2年（[[1262年]]）の社録に「二所二俣神社」と記載されており、『延喜式神名帳』に記される「[[周防国]][[都濃郡]] 二俣神社」の論社の一社となっている（他に論社が2社ある）。[[大内氏]]が[[元亨]]元年（[[1321年]]）に社房を建立し、[[毛利氏]]は御祈祷所に指定している。明治初年には郷社に列格した。 山田神社は、旧称を'''山田権前社'''という。伊勢国山田から勧請を受けて創建されたと伝えられ、『[[日本文徳天皇実録|文徳実録]]』元慶2年6月23日条に「周防国正六位上赤田神山田神并授従五位下」と記載される国史見在社である（ただし、この「山田神」については[[大島郡]][[周防大島町]]の[[山田神社 (周防大島町)|山田神社]]も論社となっている）。明治初年には村社に列格した。 明治40年（[[1907年]]）に二所神社と山田神社が合併し、現社名になった。 明治時代の当社宮司・宮本重胤は、女性の自立のための全国組織「敬神婦人会」を設立した。明治39年（[[1906年]]）にその機関誌『女子道』を発刊し、その資金源としておみくじの製造が開始され、現在はトップシェアとなっている。また、おみくじの自動頒布機を考案したのも同社である。 {{デフォルトソート:にしよやまたしんしや}} [[Category:山口県の神社]] [[Category:式内小社]] [[Category:国史見在社]] [[Category:郷社]]

{{神社 |名称=二所山田神社 |所在地=山口県周南市大字鹿野上2894 |祭神=八千矛神・大物主神（二所神社）

{{神社 |名称=二所山田神社 |所在地=山口県周南市大字鹿野上2894 |祭神=八千矛神・大物主神（二所神社）
天照大神・豊受大神（山田神社） |社格=式内社（二所神社）・国史見在社（山田神社）・郷社 |本殿=[[流造]] }} '''二所山田神社'''（にしょやまだじんじゃ）は、[[山口県]][[周南市]]にある[[神社]]である。[[式内社]]で、旧社格は[[郷社]]。 明治時代に二所神社と山田神社が[[神社合祀|合併]]してできた神社で、旧二所神社の祭神は[[大国主|八千矛神]]・[[大物主|大物主神]]、旧山田神社の祭神は[[天照大神]]・[[トヨウケビメ|豊受大神]]である。 当社が設立した月刊新聞社「女子道社」は、日本全国の社寺で授与される[[おみくじ]]の約6割を製造している。 ==歴史== 二所神社は、旧称を'''二所大明神'''といい、社伝によればは[[昌泰]]2年（[[899年]]）の創建である。[[弘長]]2年（[[1262年]]）の社録に「二所二俣神社」と記載されており、『延喜式神名帳』に記される「[[周防国]][[都濃郡]] 二俣神社」の論社の一社となっている（他に論社が2社ある）。[[大内氏]]が[[元亨]]元年（[[1321年]]）に社房を建立し、[[毛利氏]]は御祈祷所に指定している。明治初年には郷社に列格した。 山田神社は、旧称を'''山田権前社'''という。伊勢国山田から勧請を受けて創建されたと伝えられ、『[[日本文徳天皇実録|文徳実録]]』元慶2年6月23日条に「周防国正六位上赤田神山田神并授従五位下」と記載される国史見在社である（ただし、この「山田神」については[[大島郡 (山口県)|大島郡]][[周防大島町]]の[[山田神社 (周防大島町)|山田神社]]も論社となっている）。明治初年には村社に列格した。 明治40年（[[1907年]]）に二所神社と山田神社が合併し、現社名になった。 明治時代の当社宮司・宮本重胤は、女性の自立のための全国組織「大日本敬神婦人会」を設立した。明治39年（[[1906年]]）にその機関誌『女子道』を発刊し、その資金源として[[おみくじ]]の製造が開始され、現在は社寺おみくじの占有率70%とトップシェアとなっている。また、おみくじの自動頒布機を考案したのも同社である。 {{デフォルトソート:にしよやまたしんしや}} [[Category:山口県の神社]] [[Category:周防国の式内社]] [[Category:国史見在社]] [[Category:山口県の旧郷社]]

天照大神・豊受大神（山田神社） |社格=式内社（二所神社）・国史見在社（山田神社）・郷社 |本殿=[[流造]] }} '''二所山田神社'''（にしょやまだじんじゃ）は、[[山口県]][[周南市]]にある[[神社]]である。[[式内社]]で、旧社格は[[郷社]]。 明治時代に二所神社と山田神社が[[神社合祀|合併]]してできた神社で、旧二所神社の祭神は[[大国主|八千矛神]]・[[大物主|大物主神]]、旧山田神社の祭神は[[天照大神]]・[[トヨウケビメ|豊受大神]]である。 当社が設立した月刊新聞社「女子道社」は、日本全国の社寺で授与される[[おみくじ]]の約6割を製造している。 ==歴史== 二所神社は、旧称を'''二所大明神'''といい、社伝によればは[[昌泰]]2年（[[899年]]）の創建である。[[弘長]]2年（[[1262年]]）の社録に「二所二俣神社」と記載されており、『延喜式神名帳』に記される「[[周防国]][[都濃郡]] 二俣神社」の論社の一社となっている（他に論社が2社ある）。[[大内氏]]が[[元亨]]元年（[[1321年]]）に社房を建立し、[[毛利氏]]は御祈祷所に指定している。明治初年には郷社に列格した。 山田神社は、旧称を'''山田権前社'''という。伊勢国山田から勧請を受けて創建されたと伝えられ、『[[日本文徳天皇実録|文徳実録]]』元慶2年6月23日条に「周防国正六位上赤田神山田神并授従五位下」と記載される国史見在社である（ただし、この「山田神」については[[大島郡 (山口県)|大島郡]][[周防大島町]]の[[山田神社 (周防大島町)|山田神社]]も論社となっている）。明治初年には村社に列格した。 明治40年（[[1907年]]）に二所神社と山田神社が合併し、現社名になった。 明治時代の当社宮司・宮本重胤は、女性の自立のための全国組織「大日本敬神婦人会」を設立した。明治39年（[[1906年]]）にその機関誌『女子道』を発刊し、その資金源として[[おみくじ]]の製造が開始され、現在は社寺おみくじの占有率70%とトップシェアとなっている。また、おみくじの自動頒布機を考案したのも同社である。 {{デフォルトソート:にしよやまたしんしや}} [[Category:山口県の神社]] [[Category:周防国の式内社]] [[Category:国史見在社]] [[Category:山口県の旧郷社]]

{{Shrine |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = [[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{coord|34|14|2.97|N|131|49|0.49|E}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} (Nisho Shrine)
{{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} |main_hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the Modern shrine ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}}'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}, who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the early Meiji period, it was ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}. It is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}, entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in {{ill|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi|ja|周防大島町}} is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == Related Topics == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神}} * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:History of Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gōsha of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[ja:二所山田神社]]

The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}}'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}, who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the early Meiji period, it was ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}. It is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}, entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in {{ill|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi|ja|周防大島町}} is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} |main_hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the Modern shrine ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}}'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}, who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the early Meiji period, it was ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}. It is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}, entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in {{ill|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi|ja|周防大島町}} is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}. It is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}, entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in {{ill|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi|ja|周防大島町}} is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. {{Shrine |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = [[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{coord|34|14|2.97|N|131|49|0.49|E}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} (Nisho Shrine)

It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the Modern shrine ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神}}. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}}'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}, who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the early Meiji period, it was ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}. It is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}, entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in {{ill|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi|ja|周防大島町}} is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. {{Shrine |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = [[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{coord|34|14|2.97|N|131|49|0.49|E}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} (Nisho Shrine)
{{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} |main_hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the Modern shrine ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}}'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}, who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the early Meiji period, it was ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}. It is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}, entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in {{ill|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi|ja|周防大島町}} is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}}'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}, who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the early Meiji period, it was ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}. It is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}, entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in {{ill|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi|ja|周防大島町}} is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. {{Shrine |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = [[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{coord|34|14|2.97|N|131|49|0.49|E}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} (Nisho Shrine)
{{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} |main_hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the Modern shrine ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}}'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}, who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the early Meiji period, it was ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}. It is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}, entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in {{ill|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi|ja|周防大島町}} is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. {{Shrine |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = [[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{coord|34|14|2.97|N|131|49|0.49|E}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} (Nisho Shrine)
{{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} |main_hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the Modern shrine ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}}'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}, who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the early Meiji period, it was ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}. It is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}, entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in {{ill|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi|ja|周防大島町}} is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. {{Shrine |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = [[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{coord|34|14|2.97|N|131|49|0.49|E}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} (Nisho Shrine)
{{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} |main_hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the Modern shrine ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}}'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}, who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the early Meiji period, it was ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}. It is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}, entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in {{ill|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi|ja|周防大島町}} is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神}} * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:History of Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gōsha of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]] ]] [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:isho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:isho Yamada Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]]

Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}. It is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}, entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in {{ill|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi|ja|周防大島町}} is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. {{Shrine |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = [[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{coord|34|14|2.97|N|131|49|0.49|E}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} (Nisho Shrine)
{{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} |main_hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the Modern shrine ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}}'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}, who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the early Meiji period, it was ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}. It is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}, entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in {{ill|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi|ja|周防大島町}} is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. revision.text:
{{Shrine |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = [[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{coord|34|14|2.97|N|131|49|0.49|E}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} (Nisho Shrine)
{{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} |main_hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} and was formerly ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the Modern shrine ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳}} for {{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}}'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}, who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the early Meiji period, it was ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}. It is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}, entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in {{ill|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi|ja|周防大島町}} is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神}} * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:History of Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gōsha of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category: tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]] ]] [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:isho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:isho Yamada Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]]

{{Shrine |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = [[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県|12=draft}}, Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{coord|34|14|2.97|N|131|49|0.49|E}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|12=draft}} (Nisho Shrine)
{{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|12=draft}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|12=draft}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft}} |main_hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|12=draft}} }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県|12=draft}}, Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} and was formerly ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft}} under the Modern shrine ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|12=draft}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|12=draft}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ|12=draft}} distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}} for {{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国|12=draft}}'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏|12=draft}}, who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏|12=draft}}. In the early Meiji period, it was ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft}}. It is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録|12=draft}}, entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|12=draft}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in {{ill|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi|ja|周防大島町|12=draft}} is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ|12=draft}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:History of Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gōsha of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] }} [[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[en:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[sn:isho Yamada Shrine]]

During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ|12=draft}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. revision.comment: Text replacement - "|天照大神|" to "|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|"
revision.text:
{{Shrine |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = [[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県|12=draft}}, Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{coord|34|14|2.97|N|131|49|0.49|E}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|12=draft}} (Nisho Shrine)
{{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|12=draft}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|12=draft}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft}} |main_hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|12=draft}} }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県|12=draft}}, Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} and was formerly ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft}} under the Modern shrine ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|12=draft}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|12=draft}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ|12=draft}} distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}} for {{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国|12=draft}}'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏|12=draft}}, who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏|12=draft}}. In the early Meiji period, it was ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft}}. It is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録|12=draft}}, entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|12=draft}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in {{ill|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi|ja|周防大島町|12=draft}} is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ|12=draft}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:History of Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gōsha of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[en:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[sn:isho Yamada Shrine]]

{{Shrine |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = [[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{coord|34|14|2.97|N|131|49|0.49|E}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|12=draft|12=draft}} (Nisho Shrine)
{{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|12=draft|12=draft}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|12=draft|12=draft}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft|12=draft}} |main_hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|12=draft|12=draft}} }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県|12=draft|12=draft}}, Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft}} and was formerly Ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft|12=draft}} under the Modern shrine Ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|12=draft|12=draft}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|12=draft|12=draft}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ|12=draft|12=draft}} distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=draft|12=draft}} for {{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国|12=draft|12=draft}}'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏|12=draft|12=draft}}. In the early Meiji period, it was Ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録|12=draft|12=draft}}, entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in {{ill|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi|ja|周防大島町|12=draft|12=draft}} is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was Ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ|12=draft|12=draft}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:History of Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gōsha of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[en:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[sn:isho Yamada Shrine]]

{{Shrine |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = [[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{coord|34|14|2.97|N|131|49|0.49|E}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} (Nisho Shrine)
{{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} |main_hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha}} and was formerly Ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} under the Modern shrine Ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho}} for {{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国}}'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏}}, who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏}}. In the early Meiji period, it was Ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}. It is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}, entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in {{ill|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi|ja|周防大島町}} is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was Ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}} * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:History of Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gōsha of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[en:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[sn:isho Yamada Shrine]]

{{Shrine |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = [[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県}}, Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{coord|34|14|2.97|N|131|49|0.49|E}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神}} (Nisho Shrine)

revision.comment: 17 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:二所山田神社 up to rev 92436236
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称=二所山田神社 |画像=[[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |所在地=山口県周南市大字鹿野上2894 |ISO = JP-35 |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 14|緯度秒 = 2.97 |経度度 = 131|経度分 = 49|経度秒 = 0.49 |ラベル位置 = left |地図 = Japan Yamaguchi |祭神=八千矛神・大物主神（二所神社）
天照大神・豊受大神（山田神社） |社格=式内社（二所神社）・国史見在社（山田神社）・郷社 |本殿=[[流造]] }} '''二所山田神社'''（にしょやまだじんじゃ）は、[[山口県]][[周南市]]にある[[神社]]である。[[式内社]]で、旧社格は[[郷社]]。 明治時代に二所神社と山田神社が[[神社合祀|合併]]してできた神社で、旧二所神社の祭神は[[大国主|八千矛神]]・[[大物主|大物主神]]、旧山田神社の祭神は[[天照大神]]・[[トヨウケビメ|豊受大神]]である。 当社が設立した月刊新聞社「女子道社」は、日本全国の社寺で授与される[[おみくじ]]の約7割を製造している。 ==歴史== 二所神社は、旧称を'''二所大明神'''といい、社伝によれば[[昌泰]]2年（[[899年]]）の創建である。[[弘長]]2年（[[1262年]]）の社録に「二所二俣神社」と記載されており、『延喜式神名帳』に記される「[[周防国]][[都濃郡]] 二俣神社」の論社の一社となっている（他に論社が2社ある）。[[大内氏]]が[[元亨]]元年（[[1321年]]）に社房を建立し、[[毛利氏]]は御祈祷所に指定している。明治初年には郷社に列格した。 山田神社は、旧称を'''山田権前社'''という。伊勢国山田から勧請を受けて創建されたと伝えられ、『[[日本文徳天皇実録|文徳実録]]』元慶2年6月23日条に「周防国正六位上赤田神山田神并授従五位下」と記載される国史見在社である（ただし、この「山田神」については[[大島郡 (山口県)|大島郡]][[周防大島町]]の[[山田神社 (周防大島町)|山田神社]]も論社となっている）。明治初年には村社に列格した。 明治40年（[[1907年]]）に二所神社と山田神社が合併し、現社名になった。 明治時代の当社宮司・宮本重胤は、女性の自立のための全国組織「大日本敬神婦人会」を設立した。明治39年（[[1906年]]）にその機関誌『女子道』を発刊し、その資金源として[[おみくじ]]の製造が開始され、現在は社寺おみくじの占有率70%とトップシェアとなっている。また、おみくじの自動頒布機（[[自動販売機]]）を考案したのも同社である。 Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|女子道碑 Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|女子道碑の説明文 == 関連項目 == * [[天照大御神]] * [[神道]] * [[神社]] == 外部リンク == *[http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ 二所山田神社] {{神道 横}} {{デフォルトソート:にしよやまたしんしや}} [[Category:山口県の神社]] [[Category:周南市の建築物]] [[Category:周南市の歴史]] [[Category:周防国の式内社]] [[Category:国史見在社]] [[Category:山口県の旧郷社]]

{{神社 |名称=二所山田神社 |画像=[[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |所在地=山口県周南市大字鹿野上2894 |ISO = JP-35 |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 14|緯度秒 = 2.97 |経度度 = 131|経度分 = 49|経度秒 = 0.49 |ラベル位置 = left |地図 = Japan Yamaguchi |祭神=八千矛神・大物主神（二所神社）
天照大神・豊受大神（山田神社） |社格=式内社（二所神社）・国史見在社（山田神社）・郷社 |本殿=[[流造]] }} '''二所山田神社'''（にしょやまだじんじゃ）は、[[山口県]][[周南市]]にある[[神社]]である。[[式内社]]で、旧社格は[[郷社]]。 明治時代に二所神社と山田神社が[[神社合祀|合併]]してできた神社で、旧二所神社の祭神は[[大国主|八千矛神]]・[[大物主|大物主神]]、旧山田神社の祭神は[[天照大神]]・[[トヨウケビメ|豊受大神]]である。 当社が設立した月刊新聞社「女子道社」は、日本全国の社寺で授与される[[おみくじ]]の約7割を製造している。 ==歴史== 二所神社は、旧称を'''二所大明神'''といい、社伝によれば[[昌泰]]2年（[[899年]]）の創建である。[[弘長]]2年（[[1262年]]）の社録に「二所二俣神社」と記載されており、『延喜式神名帳』に記される「[[周防国]][[都濃郡]] 二俣神社」の論社の一社となっている（他に論社が2社ある）。[[大内氏]]が[[元亨]]元年（[[1321年]]）に社房を建立し、[[毛利氏]]は御祈祷所に指定している。明治初年には郷社に列格した。 山田神社は、旧称を'''山田権前社'''という。伊勢国山田から勧請を受けて創建されたと伝えられ、『[[日本文徳天皇実録|文徳実録]]』元慶2年6月23日条に「周防国正六位上赤田神山田神并授従五位下」と記載される国史見在社である（ただし、この「山田神」については[[大島郡 (山口県)|大島郡]][[周防大島町]]の[[山田神社 (周防大島町)|山田神社]]も論社となっている）。明治初年には村社に列格した。 明治40年（[[1907年]]）に二所神社と山田神社が合併し、現社名になった。 明治時代の当社宮司・宮本重胤は、女性の自立のための全国組織「大日本敬神婦人会」を設立した。明治39年（[[1906年]]）にその機関誌『女子道』を発刊し、その資金源として[[おみくじ]]の製造が開始され、現在は社寺おみくじの占有率70%とトップシェアとなっている。また、おみくじの自動頒布機（[[自動販売機]]）を考案したのも同社である。 Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|女子道碑 Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|女子道碑の説明文 == 関連項目 == * [[天照大御神]] * [[神道]] * [[神社]] == 外部リンク == *[http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ 二所山田神社] {{神道 横}} {{デフォルトソート:にしよやまたしんしや}} [[Category:山口県の神社]] [[Category:周南市の建築物]] [[Category:周南市の歴史]] [[Category:周防国の式内社]] [[Category:国史見在社]] [[Category:山口県の旧郷社]]

In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}} * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:History of Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gōsha of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:11]] }} [[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[en:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[sn:isho Yamada Shrine]] {{translated page|ja|二所山田神社|version=92436236|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{Shrine |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = [[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県|12=draft}}, Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{coord|34|14|2.97|N|131|49|0.49|E}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|12=draft}} (Nisho Shrine)
{{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|12=draft}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|12=draft}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft}} |main_hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|12=draft}} }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県|12=draft}}, Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft}} and was formerly Ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|12=draft}} under the Modern shrine Ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|12=draft}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|12=draft}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ|12=draft}} distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|12=draft}} for {{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国|12=draft}}'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏|12=draft}}, who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏|12=draft}}. In the early Meiji period, it was Ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft}}. It is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録|12=draft}}, entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|12=draft}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in {{ill|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi|ja|周防大島町|12=draft}} is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was Ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ|12=draft}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|二所山田神社|version=92436236|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:History of Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gōsha of Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[en:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[sn:isho Yamada Shrine]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "Former Gō-sha of " to "Former Gō-sha in "
revision.text:
{{Shrine |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = [[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |location = {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県|qq=draft}}, Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{coord|34|14|2.97|N|131|49|0.49|E}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|qq=draft}} (Nisho Shrine)
{{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|qq=draft}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft}} |main_hall = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|qq=draft}} }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Shūnan, Yamaguchi|ja|周南市|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|ja|山口県|qq=draft}}, Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft}} and was formerly Ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft}} under the Modern shrine Ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|qq=draft}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|qq=draft}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ|qq=draft}} distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft}} for {{ill|Suō Province|ja|周防国|qq=draft}}'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the {{ill|Ōuchi clan|ja|大内氏|qq=draft}}, who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the {{ill|Mōri clan|ja|毛利氏|qq=draft}}. In the early Meiji period, it was Ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in {{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|qq=draft}}. It is mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録|qq=draft}}, entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in {{ill|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi|ja|周防大島町|qq=draft}} is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was Ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing {{ill|Omikuji|ja|おみくじ|qq=draft}} as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|二所山田神社|version=92436236|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:History of Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[en:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[sn:isho Yamada Shrine]]
