{{Shrine |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = [[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |location = [[:en:Shūnan|Shūnan, Yamaguchi]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{coord|34|14|2.97|N|131|49|0.49|E}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=}} (Nisho Shrine)
[[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|qq=}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=}} |main_hall = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shūnan|Shūnan, Yamaguchi]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} and was formerly Ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=}} under the Modern shrine Ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]]'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]], who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the early Meiji period, it was Ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]]. It is mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]], entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|qq=}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in [[:en:Suō-Ōshima|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi]] is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was Ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}} * [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] * [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|二所山田神社|version=92436236|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:History of Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[en:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[sn:isho Yamada Shrine]]

The shrine was supported by the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]], who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the early Meiji period, it was Ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]]. It is mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]], entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|qq=}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in [[:en:Suō-Ōshima|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi]] is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was Ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Buildings and structures in Shūnan, Yamaguchi
revision.text:
{{Shrine |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = [[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |location = [[:en:Shūnan|Shūnan, Yamaguchi]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{coord|34|14|2.97|N|131|49|0.49|E}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Nisho Shrine)
[[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} |main_hall = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shūnan|Shūnan, Yamaguchi]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} and was formerly Ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} under the Modern shrine Ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]]'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]], who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the early Meiji period, it was Ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]]. It is mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]], entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in [[:en:Suō-Ōshima|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi]] is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was Ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} * [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] * [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|二所山田神社|version=92436236|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Shūnan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[sn:isho Yamada Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Shunan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:周南市の歴史]] {{Commons category|Nisho-Yamada-jinja}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "市の歴史" to "の Municipal History"
revision.text:
{{Shrine |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = [[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |location = [[:en:Shūnan|Shūnan, Yamaguchi]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{coord|34|14|2.97|N|131|49|0.49|E}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Nisho Shrine)
[[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#District_shrine|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} |main_hall = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shūnan|Shūnan, Yamaguchi]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} and was formerly Ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#District_shrine|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} under the Modern shrine Ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]]'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]], who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the early Meiji period, it was Ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]]. It is mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]], entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in [[:en:Suō-Ōshima|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi]] is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was Ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} * [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] * [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|二所山田神社|version=92436236|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[sn:isho Yamada Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Shunan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:周南の Municipal History]] {{Commons category|Nisho-Yamada-jinja}}

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11371572]] Wikidata name: Nisho yamada-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Shrine |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = [[File:Nisyoyamada_shrine.jpg|270px]] |location = [[:en:Shūnan|Shūnan, Yamaguchi]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{coord|34|14|2.97|N|131|49|0.49|E}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Nisho Shrine)
[[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#District_shrine|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} |main_hall = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shūnan|Shūnan, Yamaguchi]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and was formerly Ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#District_shrine|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} under the Modern shrine Ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]]'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]], who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the early Meiji period, it was Ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]]. It is mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]], entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in [[:en:Suō-Ōshima|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi]] is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was Ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} * [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] * [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|二所山田神社|version=92436236|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[sn:isho Yamada Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Shunan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:History of Shunan City]] {{Commons category|Nisho-Yamada-jinja}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11371572]] Wikidata name: Nisho yamada-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = File:Nisyoyamada shrine.jpg |location = [[:en:Shūnan|Shūnan, Yamaguchi]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{Coord|34.2341583|N|131.8168028|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Nisho Shrine)
[[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#District_shrine|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} |main_hall = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] |location = {{ill|Shunan|qid=Q643929}} |architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|qid=Q210684}} }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shūnan|Shūnan, Yamaguchi]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and was formerly Ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#District_shrine|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} under the Modern shrine Ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]]'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]], who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the early Meiji period, it was Ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]]. It is mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]], entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in [[:en:Suō-Ōshima|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi]] is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was Ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} * [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] * [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|二所山田神社|version=92436236|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[sn:isho Yamada Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Shunan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:History of Shunan City]] {{Commons category|Nisho-Yamada-jinja}}

It is mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]], entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. Yamada Shrine was Ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} * [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] * [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|二所山田神社|version=92436236|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[sn:isho Yamada Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Shunan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:History of Shunan City]] {{Commons category|Nisho-Yamada-jinja}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11371572]] Wikidata name: Nisho yamada-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = File:Nisyoyamada shrine.jpg |location = [[:en:Shūnan|Shūnan, Yamaguchi]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{Coord|34.2341583|N|131.8168028|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Nisho Shrine)
[[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |main_hall = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] |location = {{ill|Shunan|qid=Q643929}} |architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|qid=Q210684}} }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shūnan|Shūnan, Yamaguchi]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and was formerly Ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the Modern shrine Ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]]'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]], who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the early Meiji period, it was Ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]]. It is mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]], entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in [[:en:Suō-Ōshima|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi]] is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was Ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} * [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] * [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|二所山田神社|version=92436236|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[sn:isho Yamada Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Shunan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:History of Shunan City]] {{Commons category|Nisho-Yamada-jinja}}

It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and was formerly Ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the Modern shrine Ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]]'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]], who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the early Meiji period, it was Ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]]. It is mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]], entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in [[:en:Suō-Ōshima|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi]] is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was Ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} * [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] * [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|二所山田神社|version=92436236|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[sn:isho Yamada Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Shunan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:History of Shunan City]] {{Commons category|Nisho-Yamada-jinja}} [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11371572]] Wikidata name: Nisho yamada-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = File:Nisyoyamada shrine.jpg |location = [[:en:Shūnan|Shūnan, Yamaguchi]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{Coord|34.2341583|N|131.8168028|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Nisho Shrine)
[[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |main_hall = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] |location = {{ill|Shunan|qid=Q643929}} |architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|qid=Q210684}} }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shūnan|Shūnan, Yamaguchi]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and was formerly Ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the Modern shrine Ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]]'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]], who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the early Meiji period, it was Ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]]. It is mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]], entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in [[:en:Suō-Ōshima|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi]] is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was Ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} * [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] * [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|二所山田神社|version=92436236|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Nisho Yamada Shrine]] [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[sn:isho Yamada Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Shunan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:History of Shunan City]] {{Commons category|Nisho-Yamada-jinja}}

The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]]'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]], who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the early Meiji period, it was Ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]]. It is mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]], entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in [[:en:Suō-Ōshima|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi]] is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was Ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and was formerly Ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the Modern shrine Ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]]'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]], who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the early Meiji period, it was Ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]]. It is mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]], entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in [[:en:Suō-Ōshima|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi]] is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was Ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} * [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] * [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|二所山田神社|version=92436236|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[Category:Buildings in Shunan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:History of Shunan City]] {{Commons category|Nisho-Yamada-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11371572}}

In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} * [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] * [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|二所山田神社|version=92436236|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[Category:Buildings in Shunan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:History of Shunan City]] {{Commons category|Nisho-Yamada-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11371572}}

[[sn:commons:Category:Nisho-Yamada-jinja]][[ja:二所山田神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11371572]] Wikidata name: Nisho yamada-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = File:Nisyoyamada shrine.jpg |location = [[:en:Shūnan|Shūnan, Yamaguchi]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{Coord|34.2341583|N|131.8168028|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Nisho Shrine)
[[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |main_hall = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] |location = {{ill|Shunan|WD=Q643929}} |architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shūnan|Shūnan, Yamaguchi]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and was formerly Ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the Modern shrine Ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]]'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]], who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the early Meiji period, it was Ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]]. It is mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]], entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in [[:en:Suō-Ōshima|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi]] is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was Ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_1.jpg|Monument to Joshi-dō Nisyoyamada_shrine_jyoshido_2.jpg|Explanation plaque for the Joshi-dō monument == See Also == * {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} * [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] * [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] == External Links == * [http://www.nishoyamada.jp/ Nishoyamada Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|二所山田神社|version=92436236|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suō Province]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Yamaguchi Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[ja:二所山田神社]] [[Category:Buildings in Shunan, Yamaguchi]] [[Category:History of Shunan City]] {{Commons category|Nisho-Yamada-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11371572}}

The shrine was supported by the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]], who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the early Meiji period, it was Ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]]. It is mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]], entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in [[:en:Suō-Ōshima|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi]] is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was Ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]]'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]], who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the early Meiji period, it was Ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]]. It is mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]], entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in [[:en:Suō-Ōshima|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi]] is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was Ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines. [[sn:commons:Category:Nisho-Yamada-jinja]][[ja:二所山田神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11371572]] Wikidata name: Nisho yamada-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Nishoyamada Shrine |image = File:Nisyoyamada shrine.jpg |location = [[:en:Shūnan|Shūnan, Yamaguchi]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan |ISO = JP-35 |coordinates = {{Coord|34.2341583|N|131.8168028|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |label_position = left |map = Japan Yamaguchi |enshrined_deity = {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}} (Nisho Shrine)
[[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}} (Yamada Shrine) |shrine_Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Nisho Shrine)・{{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Yamada Shrine)・Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |main_hall = [[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]] |location = {{ill|Shunan|WD=Q643929}} |architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }} '''Nishoyamada Shrine''' (二所山田神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Shūnan|Shūnan, Yamaguchi]], [[:en:Yamaguchi Prefecture|Yamaguchi Prefecture]], Japan. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} and was formerly Ranked as a {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the Modern shrine Ranking system. The shrine was established through the merger of Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine during the Meiji period. The deities enshrined at the former Nisho Shrine are {{ill|Yachihoko|ja|八千矛神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yachihoko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大物主}}, while those of the former Yamada Shrine are [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] and {{ill|Toyouke-Ōmikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-Ōmikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}}. The shrine operates a monthly publication called "Joshi-dō-sha" (女子道社), which manufactures approximately 70% of the [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] distributed at shrines and temples throughout Japan. == History == Nisho Shrine, originally known as "Nisho Daimyōjin," is said to have been founded in 899 (Shōtai 2) according to shrine tradition. A record from 1262 (Kōchō 2) refers to it as "Nisho Futamata Shrine," and it is considered one of the possible sites of the "Futamata Shrine" listed in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} for [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]]'s Tō District, alongside two other candidate shrines. The shrine was supported by the [[:en:Ōuchi clan|Ōuchi clan]], who constructed shrine buildings in 1321 (Genkō 1), and designated as a place of prayer by the [[:en:Mōri clan|Mōri clan]]. In the early Meiji period, it was Ranked as a Gōsha. Yamada Shrine, formerly called "Yamada Gonzen-sha," is said to have been founded following a deity transfer from Yamada in [[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]]. It is mentioned in the [[:en:Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku]], entry for June 23, 878 (Gangyō 2), as "Yamada Shrine of Akada, Suō Province," making it a {{ill|Kokushi genzaisha|ja|国史見在社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi genzaisha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 社格|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. However, another Yamada Shrine in [[:en:Suō-Ōshima|Suo-Oshima, Yamaguchi]] is also considered a candidate for this historical reference. Yamada Shrine was Ranked as a Village Shrine in the early Meiji period. In 1907 (Meiji 40), Nisho Shrine and Yamada Shrine merged to form the present Nishoyamada Shrine. During the Meiji period, the shrine's chief priest, Miyamoto Shigetane, established the nationwide women's organization "Greater Japan Respect-for-the-Deities Women's Association" (大日本敬神婦人会) to promote women's independence. In 1906 (Meiji 39), he launched the association’s magazine, ''Joshi-dō'' (女子道), and began manufacturing [[:en:O-mikuji|Omikuji]] as a source of funding. Today, the shrine's affiliated organization produces about 70% of all omikuji in Japan and even developed the first omikuji vending machines.
